segments.text: Morning.
Morning. Welcome, welcome. Good to see you all.
And we will, we're obviously, we're faring our way
through the briefing.
It's about is our third go, I think. It isn't.
Um, look, uh, welcome.
So, um, if you quickly introduce yourselves
and we'll just carry on from we left off team.
Great. Thanks.
Excellent. Thanks, Mr. Chair.
Um, and committee members, this is the, this
Is the, Hmm. Long-term
Insights. Long-term Insights Briefing.
Yeah. Yeah. So our first,
First one, first field. Okay.
So, um, thanks Chair
and committee members for the opportunity to speak
to you today on our long term Insights Briefing,
building New Zealand's long-term Resilience to Hazards.
My name is Ben King. I'm the Secretary
and Chief Executive of the Department
of the Prime Minister and Cabinet.
I'm here with my colleague, James Palmer, who is, uh, the,
the Secretary and Chief Executive for Environment
and the Ministry for Environment.
Um, while this is my first time, uh,
before this committee, I'd like
to acknowledge the bipartisan approach, uh,
that is taken in general on emergency
management and national resilience.
The committee I usually report to the, uh,
government Administration Committee, um, the chair, uh,
Camilla Blich and Mr. Mitchell have a really good work
relationship building allege, uh, their approach.
And I was delighted that James tells me, um,
it's a similar approach here.
I'd like to start by acknowledging, uh, those
who tragically lost their lives in January's, uh,
weather events, our thoughts, uh,
with those communities affected.
And I'd also like to acknowledge the impact weather events
more recently, uh, across the Lower North Island, while
how long-term Insights Briefing
was prepared before these events.
Uh, they do bring into focus the stark reality
of the challenges we face as an agent, in line
with the intent and guidance
for long-term insights briefings.
Our briefing, uh, was produced independently of ministers
and does not comment on government policy
or provide recommendations.
So it's a think piece, a piece of assessment intended
to look beyond the immediate political horizon
and help us all parliament, the public
and the private sector plan for future challenges.
The focus of our briefing is national resilience.
We define this as the ability to prevent, minimize, absorb,
adapt to recover from, and transform through shocks
and stresses resilience
and enhance safety, security, prosperity of our people.
Historically, new Zealand's approach
to risk management has been largely reactive.
However, we know that human
and financial costs of that model, uh, unacceptably high.
So our briefing argues for a strategic, proactive approach,
making the investments
and the hard choices now that will prevent loss
and harm in the future.
To be proactive, we must be clear-eyed about the landscape.
We are in New Zealand as a multi-aged environment,
and we are consistently ranked amongst the countries
with the highest disaster risk in the world.
National Risk Register identifies 14 major hazards,
but in this briefing we have mostly focused on the big six,
those hazards that either have the potential
for catastrophic impact
or occur so frequently
that the accumulative cost is immense.
DPMC takes the challenges outlined in this briefing.
Seriously, over the last year,
we have reprioritized our resourcing, allowing us
to increase the number of staff we have dedicated
to implementing the National Risk and Resilience Framework.
From three to 13,
these staff are focused on working across government
to proactively build resilience.
And late last year, we've supported the Prime Minister
to deliver the first ever national Risk
and Resilience report back to cabinet,
which identified opportunities
to strengthen accountability and oversight.
We also reshaped the governance system, which, uh,
which oversees the, uh, national Resilience System, uh,
with the National Security Board
and Emergency Management Board,
and a National Hazard Risk Reduction Board reporting into a
National Resilience Board as the umbrella.
Well done. Thanks very much for the opportunity
to appear before you today.
Um, we look forward to the discussion
of the, of the Briefing.
And pass now to James.
Thank you being Koto, uh, chair committee members.
Um, thanks Ben for the introduction
and also acknowledging the tragic loss of life, uh,
and the ongoing disruption caused

the D-P-M-C-N-M-E fee team who developed this briefing, uh,
a number of whom are with us, uh, today,
and just c in the insights that they've provided,
and also the way in which the agencies, uh,
have worked together, uh, for the ministry.
Working with DPMC, uh,
on this has been a particular, uh, privilege.

the public sector was mobilized, uh,
once again alongside communities to deal, uh,
the latest real life demonstration of the very issues
that raised, and that was, uh, severe weather, uh, in the,
uh, Nelson Tasman, uh, district.
And throughout the time that we've developed this, uh,
and there have been ongoing events, I've been struck
by two key thoughts.
The first of which is that as a country, we need
to stop being surprised.
Mm-hmm. Uh, events like these are not anomalies.
Um, they're known features of life in New Zealand.
Uh, they've had them many times in the past,
and they'll continue, uh, to have a science has told us
for decades that as a small island nation,
nations straddling a tectonic plate boundary
and surrounded by warming oceans.
Our hazard profile is not a matter of if,
but it is when second,
there is an enormous opportunity in that predictability.
If we accept the certainty of these events,
we can become one of the most prepared nations in the world.
New Zealand currently loses 0.6%
of GDP annually on average to natural hazards.
That's nearly twice the OECD average.
Since 2010, we've spent $64 billion
as a country on hazard related costs,
and 97% of that expenditure has been on response
and recovery, and only 3% has been spent on
reducing risk in the first place.
That balance, uh, is unsustainable
and that core concept we wanted to highlight, uh,
with this briefing, how do we shift
as a country from being reactive

Uh, the big six hazards, the major, uh, risks

to rupture in ways that could radically transform our cities

severe weather has become the hazard we see and feel.

and how we've built our homes, roads, and infrastructure.

reminding us that biological threats can escalate quickly

One. Truth is constant.
A disaster only occurs when a hazard meets people assets
and the systems that are exposed and vulnerable.
We cannot stop the earth from moving
or the weather from intensifying at least, uh,
in the short to medium term.
But we can shape what is placed in harm's way

That requires systems, legal planning, financial, digital,

they're all fragmented, aging,

A risk profile is shaped by broader forces.
Some that increase our exposure

our development patterns.
Most of our towns and cities built on river mouths

when they are nurtured.

Our systems must be designed
with this interconnectedness in mind.

unavoidable choices ahead.

The National Adaptation plan,

Uh, New Zealand is one where people

Resilience is a long game, and it requires all

The future does not simply happen to us.
It's something that we can shape together.

source_metadata.description:
Thursday, 19th February 2026 | Environment Committee

Ministry for the Environment and Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, Long-term Insights Briefing 2025

Hearing of evidence:

Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet
Ben King, Chief Executive
Sean Bolton, Deputy Chief Executive
Peter Ranger, Senior Advisor

Ministry for the Environment
James Palmer, Chief Executive
Dr Alison Collins, Chief Science Advisor
Katherine Wilson, General Manager – Adaptation
Dr Alice Cleland, Manager – Insights
Paloma Hartevelt, Acting Chief Advisor
